claim_chain: Severe infections have been linked to dementia but comorbidities may confound this association
We examined 170 diseases among 60,000+ dementia cases and 300,000+ controls using nationwide registries
We identified 29 diseases robustly associated with dementia including 2 severe infections
Less than one-seventh of excess dementia risk from infections was attributable to comorbidities
The associations between infections and dementia are largely independent of prior conditions
Severe infections may be independent risk factors for dementia

typical_pattern: Provide supporting evidence or detail from the data
Compare with previous literature or expectations

likely_priorities: Validity of register-based diagnoses and case ascertainment
Appropriateness of statistical methods including multiple testing correction
likely_attacks: Lack of baseline cognitive assessments and clinical examination data
Potential misclassification of dementia cases as controls due to delayed diagnosis
Missing data on specific infection treatments
Inability to assess psychosocial, behavioural, or biological confounders not captured in registries
Overly conservative Bonferroni correction may miss true associations
Analysis of all-cause dementia rather than subtypes
reviewer_preference_profile.inference_basis: The paper explicitly lists limitations including: 'baseline cognitive assessments and clinical examination data leading to dementia and other disease diagnoses were not available', 'no data on specific treatments were available', 'We examined all-cause dementia as the outcome. Future work should investigate dementia subtypes', 'We may have missed some true associations because we...applied Bonferroni correction for multiple testing, which may be overly conservative', 'Some dementia cases may have been misclassified as controls owing to delayed dementia diagnosis', and 'We were not able to directly assess psychosocial, behavioural, or biological confounders that are not captured in nationwide registries.'

may accelerate pre-existing preclinical stage of dementia rather than initiate neurodegeneration
These results support the role of severe infections as independent risk factors for dementia
We speculate that the relative contribution of infections on dementia risk might be stronger

verification_status.notes: All extracted phrases are literal substrings from the input text. No external references, invented numbers, or author names were included. The profile is based solely on the provided abstract, discussion, and figure legends.
